coredata.dc:title: Defective graphene-nanomaterials derived from banana-biomass for simultaneous electrochemical detection of xanthine, hypoxanthine, and uric acid: Insights from scanning electrochemical microscopy on edge and basal planes

coredata.dc:description: While graphene and other graphene-based materials are promising for electrochemical sensors, developing a simple synthesis method and understanding how their structure affects their performance remains challenging. This study introduces a straightforward method to produce highly defective graphene-based nanomaterials (BP-GNMs) from banana pulp (BP) biomass. These BP-GNMs are suitable for efficiently detecting purine bases (xanthine (X), hypoxanthine (Hx), and uric acid (UA)) simultaneously using electrochemical techniques. The synthesis process involves heating at 180 °C in air. Compared to BP-derived graphene quantum dots (BP-GQDs) made using a hydrothermal method, BP-GNMs exhibit significantly better electrochemical activity. This is shown by well-defined, separated peaks with high currents in electrochemical measurements of the purine bases. In this study, we compared the physicochemical properties of BP-GNMs and BP-GQDs using various techniques like FESEM, TEM, XPS, UV–Vis, Raman, and FTIR spectroscopy. Our analysis revealed the formation of layered nanomaterials based on graphene, with sizes ranging from 400 mm to 10 μm. These nanomaterials exhibited a balanced ratio of sp2-bonded carbon (sp2C) to sp3-bonded carbon (sp3C). This balanced ratio is attributed to the presence of both defective basal planes (containing sp2C) and edge planes (containing sp3C) on the BP-GNMs. The heterogeneity on the surface in terms of edge and basal planes of the materials was viewed using scanning electrochemical microscopy (SECM) technique with ferrocene-carboxylic acid as a redox probe. As a practical application of this low-cost biomass material, an efficient and separation-free simultaneous electrochemical sensing of X, Hx and UA in various fishes and human urine samples were demonstrated with a recovery of 100±2%.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparative physicochemical characterizations responses of GP-GNMs and GP-GQDs by (A) and (B) FTIR, (C) UV–Vis, (D and E) Ramana spectroscopic analysis. Fig. 1A, curve a is a control FTIR response of commercial graphite nanopowder (GNP).
Comparative TEM images of (A and B) BP-GNMs and (C and D) BP-GQDs at different magnification.
Comparative FESEM images of (A, B) BP-GNMs and (C, D) BP-GQDs at different magnification.
XPS responses of C 1 s (A and B) and O 1 s (C and D) core energy levels of BP-GNMs (A and C) and BP-GQDs (B and D).

(A) Comparative CV responses of GCE (a), (b) GCE/BP-GQDs and (c) GCE/ BP-GNMs with 1 mM fc-COOOH in 0.1 M KCl solution at v = 10 mV s −1. Pictorial representation for the SECM operation procedure on (B) BP-GNMs and (C) BP-GQDs surfaces and its electron-transfer mechanism under feedback mode. SECM images of (D) GCE/BP-GNMs and (E) GCE/BP-GQDs at Etip = 700 mV and ESub = 0.2 V vs Ag/AgCl condition in 0.1 M KCl solution. Insets are illustration of the graphitic materials.

(A) Comparative CV responses of GCE, GCE/BP-GNMs and GCE/ BP-GQDs with 5 mM of K3[Fe(CN)6] in 0.1 M KCl solution at scan rate= 10 mVs−1. CV responses of (B) GCE (C) GCE/BP-GNMs and (D) GCE/BP-GQDs with 300μM each individuals of UA, X and Hx in pH 7 phosphate buffer solution at a scan rate of 10 mV s −1.

(A) Cyclic voltammograms of GCE/BP-GNMs with a mixture containing variable concentration of UA (5, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50 μM), X (40, 60, 80, 100, 120 μM) and Hx (10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60 μM) in pH 7 phosphate buffer solution at a scan rate of 20 mV s −1. Insets are typical calibration plots for (B) UA, (C) X and (D) Hx systems.
Effect of scan rate on the CV responses of GCE/BP-GNMs with 300μM each of UA (A), X (B) and Hx (C) in pH 7 phosphate buffer solution. (D-F) are respective plots of i pa vs v 1/2.
Effective pH on the DPVs responses of GCE/BP-GNMs in a mixture containing of 20 µM of each UA, X and Hx in pH 7 phosphate buffer solution. (B) Plots of E pa vs pH of UA, X and Hx.

DPV responses of GCE/BP-GNMs for simultaneous electrochemical detection of a mixture containing UA (15–40 μM), X (15–40 μM) and Hx (5–35 μM) in pH 7 phosphate buffer solution. Insets are typical calibration plots (B-D) for UA, X and Hx sensing system.
(A) DPV responses for the simultaneous responses of UA, X and Hx in a condition of detection of one analyte (10–70 μM) with other two fixed analyte concentrations (10 μM).

(A) DPV responses GCE/BP-GNMs for the simultaneous detection of UX, X and Hx in (A) Anchovy fish (#1), (B) Mackerel fish egg (#2) and (C) human urine (#4) real samples spiked without (R) and with standard concentrations (S1-S3) of a mixture of UA, X and Hx in 0.1 M PB solution (pH 7). Note: For the samples #1 and #2 known concentration of 5–20 µM of UA, X, Hx and for sample #3 known concentration of 20–80 µM of UA, X, Hx were spiked. R= Real sample, S1-S3 = Standard concentrations.
Comparative electrochemical parameters for the redox reaction of FeIII/II(CN)6 3-/4− on GCE, GCE/BP-GNMs and GCE/BP-GQDs were examined in 0.1 M KCl solution.
Comparative electrochemical parameters for the electrochemical oxidation of UA, X and Hx on GCE, GCE/BP-GNMs and GCE/BP-GQDs were examined in pH 7 PB solution by CV technique.
Comparative electroanalytical performance data for the simultaneous electrochemical sensing of UA, X and Hx on GP-GMs with existing literature.

head.ce:title: Defective graphene-nanomaterials derived from banana-biomass for simultaneous electrochemical detection of xanthine, hypoxanthine, and uric acid: Insights from scanning electrochemical microscopy on edge and basal planes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: While graphene and other graphene-based materials are promising for electrochemical sensors, developing a simple synthesis method and understanding how their structure affects their performance remains challenging. This study introduces a straightforward method to produce highly defective graphene-based nanomaterials (BP-GNMs) from banana pulp (BP) biomass. These BP-GNMs are suitable for efficiently detecting purine bases (xanthine (X), hypoxanthine (Hx), and uric acid (UA)) simultaneously using electrochemical techniques. The synthesis process involves heating at 180 °C in air. Compared to BP-derived graphene quantum dots (BP-GQDs) made using a hydrothermal method, BP-GNMs exhibit significantly better electrochemical activity. This is shown by well-defined, separated peaks with high currents in electrochemical measurements of the purine bases. In this study, we compared the physicochemical properties of BP-GNMs and BP-GQDs using various techniques like FESEM, TEM, XPS, UV–Vis, Raman, and FTIR spectroscopy. Our analysis revealed the formation of layered nanomaterials based on graphene, with sizes ranging from 400 mm to 10 μm. These nanomaterials exhibited a balanced ratio of sp2-bonded carbon (sp2C) to sp3-bonded carbon (sp3C). This balanced ratio is attributed to the presence of both defective basal planes (containing sp2C) and edge planes (containing sp3C) on the BP-GNMs. The heterogeneity on the surface in terms of edge and basal planes of the materials was viewed using scanning electrochemical microscopy (SECM) technique with ferrocene-carboxylic acid as a redox probe. As a practical application of this low-cost biomass material, an efficient and separation-free simultaneous electrochemical sensing of X, Hx and UA in various fishes and human urine samples were demonstrated with a recovery of 100±2%.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis of novel carbon nanomaterials and their useful application to real-world problems continue to be significant research interests in recent times [1–3]. Graphene, a single-atom-thick 2D material, has garnered significant attention due to its exceptional electronic, mechanical, and optical properties [4,5]. Its zero-dimensional derivative, graphene/carbon quantum dots, is noteworthy for its excellent optical and electrical characteristics [4–6]. Several synthesis methods have been developed to produce graphene (Gr), graphene oxide (GO) and graphene-quantum dots (GQDs) using different precursors and techniques such as Harmar based chemical route [7], hydrothermal [8,9], pyrolysis [10,11], microwave [12,13], and electrochemical [14,15] methods, categorized as top-down and bottom-up approaches. However, green, cost-effective, and eco-friendly methods for mass-producing highly-electroactive graphene-materials remain a research priority [3,8,12]. Utilizing biomass, including plant parts like flowers, stems, and fruits, as a primary source for Gr, GO, and GQDs production offers an effective, low-cost, and sustainable approach [3,8]. Agricultural products, particularly wastes from rice, maize, and wheat processing industries, and stems, have been utilized due to their rich composition of carbohydrates and polyphenols [8,16–18]. For instance, various methods such as hydrothermal heating and carbonization have been employed to derive carbon nanomaterials (CNs) from banana peel and stem [3,8,19–25]. It's noteworthy that banana is rich in hemicellulose with lower amounts of cellulose and lignin, making it an excellent source to produce biomass-derived carbons. These biomass-derived GQDs are highly suitable for chemical applications in supercapacitor-based electrodes [19–23]. Indeed, synthesizing electroactive and conductive Gr based materials for electrocatalytic applications with minimal capacitive background is a challenging task. In this study, biomass-derived graphene-based nanomaterials (GNMs) and water-soluble GQDs were prepared from banana pulp and explored for electrocatalytic activity towards purine metabolites: xanthine (X), hydroxanthine (Hx), and uric acid (UA) in physiological pH.

In general, the preparation of biomass-derived CNs involve procedures such as high-temperature pyrolysis at ∼800 °C, autoclave-based hydrothermal, and microwave irradiation processes [8,24]. However, each method has its own conditions in terms of yield, reaction time, defects, and surface functional groups. Moreover, these methods may yield different functional products even when using the same biomass. For instance, Zhang et al. synthesized hierarchically porous carbon nanomaterials by pyrolyzing lyophilized banana peels at 900 °C for 1 h in an Ar gas atmosphere and used it as an ideal backbone for NiCo2O4-based asymmetric supercapacitor applications [19]. Yang and Park prepared 3D porous carbon nanomaterials by activating banana peel in a 2 M KOH solution for 2 h, followed by the procedure of Zhang et al. The modified procedure leads to the formation of rich oxygen-functionalized carbon nanomaterials, especially carboxylic functional groups, which can assist in surface modification and improve supercapacitor functionality [21]. Nguyen et al. reported banana peel-based carbon quantum dots with an average size of 10 nm, prepared by the hydrothermal autoclave method, which are rich in carbonyl functional groups for supercapacitor application [23]. Recently, Padmapriya et al. demonstrated a two-step-assisted preparation of N, P-doped carbon quantum dots, wherein banana flower bract extract was subjected to hydrothermal autoclave followed by N, P doping under similar hydrothermal autoclave conditions [24]. The resulting carbon nanomaterials have been found to be an efficient system for the electrocatalytic oxidative detection of dopamine at picomolar concentrations. Therefore, it is necessary to evaluate the performance of products prepared by different methods for various applications. In this study, we developed a defective GNMs material using a gentle pyrolysis process on tender banana-pulp at mild conditions (T = 180 °C) in air for a few hours, resulting in the conversion of the pulp into brown-black colored defective carbon material. The synthesized material was designated as BP-GNMs, where BP stands for banana pulp. For comparison, we also prepared a graphene quantum dots (GQD) material using the same banana pulp subjected to hydrothermal autoclave treatment at 180 °C. This material was designated as BP-GQDs, serving as a reference for electroanalytical applications.

It is noteworthy that defective carbon-based graphene materials have recently been referred one of the most promising alternatives to the precious metal electrocatalysts due to their irreplaceable advantages, such as being environmentally friendly, cost-effective, and highly structurally tunable [26,27]. Despite remarkable progress, significant challenges remain for their further development. Generally, defective graphene structures are classified into two categories: vacancies, which create spaces within the structure, and edge defects. Vacancy defects occur when single, double, or multiple carbon atoms are missing from the graphitic structure, while edge defects involve the creation of large CO functional groups with sp3C sites [26]. In this context, in-situ doping and etching techniques using plasma, oxidants, electron beams, and thermal methods are discussed [27]. In this work, we synthesized a biomass derived-defective carbon nanomaterial by simple thermal based heating of banana-pulp.

Hx, X, and UA are important biomolecules involved in purine metabolism, playing diverse roles in maintaining cellular homeostasis and overall health [28–31]. Hx serves as an intermediate in the synthesis and degradation of nucleic acids, produced during the breakdown of adenosine monophosphate (AMP) and guanosine monophosphate (GMP), essential components of RNA and DNA. X is another purine derivative formed during the metabolism of Hx by the enzyme xanthine oxidase, serving as a precursor for the synthesis of adenine and guanine nucleotides. Xanthine can be further metabolized into the final product of purine metabolism, UA, through the action of xanthine oxidase. Uric acid is primarily excreted by the kidneys, with a small amount excreted through the gastrointestinal tract [32–34]. Therefore, simultaneous monitoring of Hx, X, and UA levels in biological systems is vital for understanding metabolic processes, diagnosing and managing diseases, evaluating kidney function, and assessing oxidative stress and inflammation. Additionally, monitoring these purine levels in deceased animals can provide insights into age-related metabolic and physiological changes [35].

To simultaneously detect X, Hx, and UA, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and electrophoresis coupled with UV–Vis techniques are often used [36,37]. However, these methods require time-consuming offline sample procedures and treatments, expensive instruments and columns, skilled lab technicians, and costly and toxic organic solvents, among other drawbacks. In contrast, electrochemical sensor-based systems offer advantages over conventional analytical techniques due to their cost-effectiveness and suitability for single-use, disposable screen-printed electrodes, and point-of-care device development. The electrochemical approach also allows for separation-free simultaneous detection of the three compounds in complex real samples. Several chemically modified electrodes (CMEs) have been reported, including SWCNT/carbon paste electrodes [38], poly(bromocresol purple) [39], Co-doped CeO2 nanoparticles [40], Ag+ deposited black phosphorene/NH2-MWCNT nanohybrids, poly(pyrocatechol violet)/functionalized multi-walled carbon nanotubes composites [41], Ru(DMSO)4Cl2 nano-aggregated Nafion membranes [42], and pre-anodized pencil graphite electrodes [43].

Recent concerns about toxic chemical-based environmental pollution and its associated carbon footprints have driven the development of eco-friendly and sustainable biomass-derived materials as alternative solutions. These materials offer a promising avenue to mitigate the adverse effects of traditional manufacturing processes, reduce environmental degradation, and promote a more sustainable future. Notably, a green material, BP-GNMs, synthesized by a simple and non-complicated thermal method in this work, exhibited excellent electrocatalytic activity and simultaneous detection functionality for X, Hx, and UA, surpassing that of BP-GQDs. The as-prepared BP-GNMs were characterized using various physicochemical and electrochemical techniques, including scanning electrochemical microscopy (SECM), and further explored for real sample analysis of X, Hx, and UA.

Bananas (Musa acuminata) were purchased from the local market in Vellore, India. Uric acid, xanthine, and hypoxanthine were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. Ethanol, anhydrous sodium dihydrogen phosphate (≥98% purity), and anhydrous disodium hydrogen phosphate (≥98% purity) were sourced from Merck and used as received. Other chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. All aqueous solutions were prepared using deionized water and alkaline KMnO4-double-distilled water. Unless otherwise specified, a pH 7 phosphate buffer (PB) solution with an ionic strength of 0.1 M, prepared by mixing stock solutions of NaH2PO4 and Na2HPO4, was used as the electrolyte in this study.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired using the FEITECNAI-G2 20 Twin instrument. For TEM analysis, the aqueous suspension of BP-GNMs and BP-GQDs was dropped onto a Cu grid. Raman spectra were recorded using an i-Raman instrument with a 532 nm laser excitation. Infrared spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu 8400S FTIR Spectrometer in ATR mode. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed using the ESCALAB 250xi XPS spectrophotometer from Thermo Scientific. The powder sample of BP-GNMs was coated on silicon tape, while the water-soluble carbon quantum dots (BP-GQDs) were drop-casted onto a silicon substrate for both Raman and XPS analysis. Electrochemical experiments were conducted using the CH Instruments Model 760C electrochemical workstation with a conventional three-electrode cell setup, comprising a modified 3 mm glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 0.0707 cm2) as the working electrode, an aqueous Ag/AgCl reference electrode, and a Pt wire counter electrode. For morphological investigation, a JSM-7610FPlus Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM) was used. For the SECM investigations, we utilized a Princeton Applied Research (PAR) VersaSCAN Potentiostat Instrument in bipotentiostat mode. A 10 μm Pt tip ultramicroelectrode (UME, Pttip) served as the sensing electrode for the probe system. A separate homemade SECM cell was employed for the modification of the glassy carbon electrode (refer to Supporting Information, Fig. S1). Prior to the experiment, we fixed the position of the probe, Pttip, against the substrate by performing an approach curve in feedback mode. This involved moving the Pttip part at a speed ranging from 1 to 100 μm/s in the z-axis direction at a fixed applied potential of 0.7 V versus Ag/AgCl, where the diffusion-controlled reduction reaction of fc-COOH occurred. Following is the optimal differential pulse voltammetric parameters used in this work; potential increment = 4 mV, amplitude = 50 mV, pulse width= 0.06 s, sample width = 0.02 s, pulse period = 0.5 s and quiet time = 2 s.

For the BP-GNMs procedure, banana pulp was first cut into small pieces. Subsequently, approximately 20 g of banana pulp was mixed with 30 mL of ethanol and thoroughly ground. The resulting banana juice was then filtered using Whatman filter paper. The filtered banana juice solution was transferred to a borosilicate glass crucible and heated on a hot plate at 70% temperature setting, with a maximum temperature of ∼180 °C, for 2 h. Initially, the solvent evaporated for few minutes, and then heating was continued for two hours in a solid state. The banana-pulp transformed into a slurry, followed by the formation of black solid particles, which were stored in a closed container at room temperature. The solid particles were subjected to various physicochemical characterizations, revealing the formation of graphene nano material. For the synthesis of BP-GQDs, a hydrothermal-autoclave based method was employed. In this process, 30 mL of filtered banana pulp-water juice was heated at 120 °C for 8 h, resulting in a brownish solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation chemically modified glassy carbon electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: The glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was polished using 0.03 µm alumina powder and then sonicated in deionized water to remove impurities. Then, the GCE was electrochemically cleaned/pre-treated by performing cyclic voltammetry in the potential window −0.2 to +0.6 V vs Ag/AgCl for 10 cycles at the scan rate of 50 mVs−1 in PBS (pH 7). Then, the 5μL of freshly prepared dispersed solution of BP-GNMs or BP-GQDs (4 mg/mL) is drop casted on above treated GCE and dried in room temperature for 10±2 min to get the respective chemically modified electrodes, GCE/BP-GNMs and GCE/BP-GQDs.

The GCE/BP-GNMs were utilized as the working electrode under differential pulse voltammetric conditions in a pH 7 PB solution. Anchovy fish (#1, Stolephorus indicus), mackerel fish egg (#2, Scomber scombrus), and shrimp (#3, Caridea) obtained from the local fish market and three healthy human urine samples collected from laboratory personnel (#4-#6), were subjected to real sample analyses. Before analysis, the collected seafood and human urine samples underwent filtration using a membrane-based micro-syringe and were then stored in a refrigerator. For the real sample analysis, 5 g of sample flesh was weighed and homogenized using a mortar and pestle with 10 mL of pH 7 PBS solution. The real samples were diluted up to 2 times with pH 7 PBS, and a known volume was spiked directly into the electrochemical cell. The standard addition approach was adopted for the quantification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of GNMs and GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The FT-IR analysis was performed to gain further insights into the structure. As shown in Fig. 1(A), the FT-IR spectra of BP-GNMs showed specific and intense peaks attributed to -OH (3350 cm−1), aromatic CHCH (2933 and 2863 cm−1), aliphatic -C = O (approximately 1728 cm−1), aromatic >C = O (approximately 1589 cm−1), and -C-O (approximately 1378 and 1199 cm−1) functional groups [44,45]. When compared with commercial graphite nano powder (GNP), which exhibited a weak IR signal due to -OH/-COOH (3310 cm−1), aromatic CHCH (2922 and 2845 cm−1), and aromatic C = O (1583 cm−1), while selectively missing aliphatic -C = O and -C-O functional groups, highly intense CO functional groups, were observed. Meanwhile, the IR spectra of the BP-GQDs were also compared with the previous samples. As shown in Fig. 1 B, intense CO functional groups due to -OH (3309 cm−1), aromatic -CHCH- (2974 and 2897 cm−1), aromatic >C = O (1654 cm−1), CO (1405 cm−1), and CH/CO (1027 cm−1) were observed. For BP-GQDs, the -OH/-COOH and aromatic >C = O were found to be relatively higher compared to GNP and BP-GNMs. The high -OH/COOH content is the reason for the high-water solubility of this material. Similarly, with BP-GNMs, aliphatic -C-OH and CO- formations are relatively higher compared to other carbon nanomaterials.

The UV–Vis spectra of the prepared carbon materials were recorded and were shown in Fig. 1C. The carbon materials prepared using the hydrothermal method (BP-GQDs) exhibit an optical absorption peak at 303 nm and strong yellowish-green luminescence under 352 nm UV light, which are significant features of the π–π* transition of the C = C band and the n–π* transition of the C = O band of BP-GQDs (Fig. 1C, curve a) [46]. The BP-GNMs showed a less intense optical absorption peak at 277 nm with a shoulder at 350 nm, attributed to the formation of a graphene-like core on the material [47].

To gain insights into the structure of the prepared materials, Raman analysis was performed with BP-GNMs and BP-GQDs as shown in Fig. 1D&E. Both samples exhibit two distinctive peaks at around 1330 cm−1 and around 1585 cm−1, which are typical disorder (D) band and crystalline (G) bands of carbonaceous structure, indicating that the prepared BP-GNMs and BP-GQDs possess both sp3 and sp2 carbon-units, respectively, albeit at different magnitudes. The intensity ratio of the D and G bands (ID/IG) is 1.2 for BP-GNMs and 0.69 for BP-GQDs, which specifies a relatively high graphitic-defect density with BP-GNMs over BP-GQDs [48]. These characteristics response implies the unique graphitic structure with CO functional groups in BP-GNMs.

Then, the nanoscale morphology of carbon materials, BP-GNMs, and BP-GQDs, was examined using the TEM technique, as shown in Fig. 2 A–D. A sheet-like structure morphology with BP-GNMs of an average size of 400 nm-10μm and dot-like morphology with a size of 50 nm to 200 nm with BP-GQDs were noticed. This observation resembles the formation of multilayer graphene sheets and quantum dots-like materials reported in the literature with graphene-oxide and GQDs systems respectively [49,50]. Extended FESEM analysis of the samples displayed in Fig. 3 A–D resembles the TEM observation. In these cases, agglomerate structures, along with the observation of sheet and tiny particle-like morphology, were noticed. The defective graphene layer was not visible in the electron-microscopic images.

The XPS analysis was conducted to understand the nature and the number of functional groups present on BP-GNMs and BP-GQDs. The original survey spectrum of BP-GNMs is shown in supporting information Figs. S2 and S3. The high-resolution XPS responses of C1s and O1s of the materials, BP-GNMs, and BP-GQDs, were displayed in Fig. 4 A-D. The C 1 s spectrum of BP-GNMs was deconvoluted into four peaks at BE, 284.6 eV (sp2C), 286.2, and 286.3 eV, corresponding to >C< (core graphitic), -C-OH/pH-OH/CO-C and >C = O/OC = O (lactone)/-COOH functional groups, respectively (Fig. 4A) [44].Similarly, the high-resolution spectrum of O 1 s of BP-GNMs was deconvoluted into five peaks at BE, 530.8, 531.8, 532.9, 533.8, and 535.7 eV, corresponding to >C = O (aromatic), >C = O (aliphatic)/-CHO, -C-OH/pH-OH, -C-OC-, -O-C = O (lactone)/H2Ochem functional groups respectively (Fig. 4B). Fig. 4C and D provide the deconvoluted high-resolution XPS responses of C1s and O1s levels of BP-GQDs. Based on the BE values, derived functional groups with C1s energy levels are; sp3C (283.3 eV), sp2C(284.6 eV), >COH/pH-OH/-C-OC/>C = O (285.9 eV) and -COOR/-COOH (288.3 eV). Furthermore, based on the O1s core energy level deconvoluted data, obtained functional groups are; >C = O (aromatic) (530.8 eV), >COH/pH-OH/CO-C (532.0 eV) and OC = O (lactone/anhydride) (533.2 eV). These functions closely match the results obtained from the IR analysis of the material (Fig. 1A and B) [51,52]. The atomic ratio of O1s/C1s was calculated by considering the binding energy window, the area under XPS peaks (C1s-284.6 and O1s-532/532.9 eV peaks), and photoionization cross-section values, resulting in a value of 0.39 for BP-GQDs and 0.013 for BP-GNMs. BP-GQDs exhibited an O1s/C1s ratio approximately 30 times higher than that of BP-GNMs. The higher C1s/O1s ratio with the BP-GNMs attributes the richness in basal plane graphitic content with fewer edge plane oxygen content [48]. The anisotropic nature of the graphitic materials can be observed by performing scanning electrochemical microscopy surface with suitable electrochemical redox probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SECM image of the electroactive sites of GCE/BP-GNMs & GCE/BP-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To understand the surface heterogeneity and the defective graphene structure, we conducted SECM analysis with BP-GNMs and BP-GQDs modified GCEs. SECM is a powerful technique that allows for in-situ imaging of electroactive sites especially basal and edge planes involved in electron-transfer reactions, unlike atomic force microscopy, which mainly provides bulk surface morphology [53–57]. Selecting the appropriate redox probe that can access the electroactive sites of the material is crucial for successful SECM studies. After several trial-and-error experiments, we determined that ferrocene carboxylic acid (fc-COOH) is the most suitable redox probe for imaging the electronic surface features of BP-GNMs and BP-GQDs. In Fig. 5 A, we present a comparative CV response of GCE/ BP-GNMs, GCE/BP-GQDs, and GCE with 1 mM fc-COOH dissolved in 0.1 M KCl solution at a scan rate of 10 mV s −1. We observed a well-defined redox peak at a standard electrode potential, Eo = 0.35 V vs Ag/AgCl, with all three electrodes, albeit with different magnitudes. The calculated i pa value with GCE/ BP-GNMs is 30 µA, which is approximately 10 and 15 times higher compared to GCE/BP-GQDs and plain GCE systems, respectively. The enhanced electronic conductivity of GCE/ BP-GNMs explains this observation. Furthermore, we noticed an i pa/i pc ratio greater than 1 with BP-GNMs, which is unusual for redox systems. This anomaly suggests strong π-π bonding interactions between the aromatic units of fc-COOH and the sp2 graphitic sites of the GNMs, accelerating the oxidation reaction. Thus, using fc-COOH as a probe offers a clear advantage in visualizing the underlying heterogeneity of graphitic carbons. In Fig. 5B, we illustrate the feedback mode of operating mechanism of SECM. The probe tip is conditioned at a potential, Etip = 0.7 V vs Ag/AgCl, where fc-COOH is oxidized to fc +-COOH ion. Subsequently, the substrate of the graphitic electrode is set at a potential, Esub = 0.2 V vs Ag/AgCl, where the fc +-COOH ion is reduced back to fc-COOH. This redox reaction occurs continuously at the substrate/tip interface, allowing for the imaging of surface heterogeneity upon the x-y movement of the tip electrode.

In Fig. 5C, we see a typical SECM image of GCE/ BP-GNMs, displaying a micro-ball-like structure with four different surface regions labeled R1 to R4, showing variable current values. Based on the tip current signals, the regions can be sequenced as R1 (14 μA) > R2 (18 μA) > R3 (10 μA) > R4 (6 μA). It's clear to see a ball-like structure (R1) of about 14 μA in current with ring-like edges (R2) of about 14 μA in the SECM image. The highest tip-current region was observed with the R2 case, attributed to the defective-basal plane site of the graphitic network structure relating to majority of vacant-graphitic carbon on the network (sp2C) with minor CO functional groups (Inset Fig. 5B and C). Similarly, appreciable current signals were noticed in the R1 region due to edge morphologies with noticeable CO functional groups (sp3C). The enhanced electrochemical response on the edge-site (sp3C) plane is common with the majority of graphitic-based carbon nanomaterials [48]. On the other hand, the lowest current region was observed in R4, attributed to basal plane sites without any CO functional groups and/or some aliphatic organic functional groups such as pyron and furan formation on the interface [44], which are electro-inactive in nature.

Fig. 5D shows the SECM response of the GCE/BP-GQDs under the mentioned experimental conditions. A very tiny ball-like structure with an average size of 50–200 nm was observed, which is consistent with the SEM analysis. It's interesting to note that the tip-current observed in this system is about 100 times lesser than that of the respective SECM image of the GCE/ BP-GNMs. The lower SECM current signal indicates the poor electronic conductivity of the BP-GQDs. The higher content of CO functional groups on the material may turn it into an insulator, thereby reducing electronic conductivity. Overall, GCE/ BP-GNMs are much better electrode materials than GCE/BP-GQDs for electrochemical and electroanalytical applications.

Based on the physicochemical, electrochemical, and SECM results, a plausible mechanism for the formation of detective GNMs can be proposed as follows: Under moderate heating conditions, the hemicellulose in the biomass slowly converts into amorphous carbon, subsequently undergoing intermediate graphitization. This is followed by a carbonization reaction, sp2C + sp3C + O2 → xCO2, as the final step. At the given optimal preparation temperature and time, a fraction of graphitic carbon converts to carbonization, i.e., sp2C + O2 → yCO2, leading to the formation of significant graphitic vacancies as defect structures. These vacancies contribute to the enhanced electrochemical activity of the BP-GNMs in this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical properties of GCE/BP-GNMs and GCE/ BP-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical properties of BP-GNMs and BP-GQDs were investigated by conducting CV in a solution of 0.1 M KCl containing a 10 mM Fe(CN)6 3-/4− redox couple at a scan rate of 10 mV/s. The GCE redox couple electron transfer was well-established in CV, with parameters such as Eo = 220 mV vs Ag/AgCl, ipa/ipc∼1, and a peak-to-peak potential difference, ΔEp = 60 mV. By studying the redox reaction on various underlying electrodes and considering the electrochemical parameters, one can obtain information about the electronic conductivity of the surface. Fig. 6 A presents a comparative CV response of GCE, GCE/ BP-GNMs, and GCE/BP-GQDs, GCE showing a qualitatively similar Eo value (220±5 mV vs Ag/AgCl) and ipa/ipc ratio (∼1), GCE/BP-GNMs showing Eo value (225±5 mV vs Ag/AgCl) and ipa/ipc ratio (∼1) but with different magnitudes of ΔEp and peak currents. The calculated ΔEp at GCE is 100 mV and for BP-GNMs is 80 mV. The lower ΔEp of BP-GNMs compared to GCE indicates the facile electron transfer properties of BP-GNMs. However, GCE/BP-GQDs showed the absence of any redox peak with the Fe(CN)6 3-/4- redox couple (Table 1 ). This observation is merely due to strong electrostatic repulsion between the anionic charge of the complex and the anionic species of the rich CO functional groups of GQDs. Consequently, we couldn't extend the use of this redox couple for the SECM imaging analysis. The redox peak current value noticed on the BP-GNMs surface is about twice as high as that on the GCE surface, indicating enhanced electronic conductivity with the BP-GNMs surface. The same experiment was performed with a positively charged metal complex, [Ru(NH3)6]2+ in a solution of 0.1 M KCl, as shown in Fig. S4. It is interesting to note that qualitatively similar voltammetric responses were observed with all three electrodes, including the negatively charged GQDs. However, the quantitative peak current values were different. The responses of the GCE and GQDs were nearly the same, while the BP-GMNs showed about twice the peak current with significant background current. Furthermore, using the Randles-Sevick equation for the reversible system [58], i pa = 2.69 × 105 A eD1/2n3/2 v 1/2 C, where i pa = anodic peak current, D = diffusion coefficient, v = scan rate, and C = concentration, the electrochemical accessible surface areas (A e) of the electrodes were calculated as 0.112 cm2 and 0.067 cm2 for BP-GNMs and GCE respectively [53]. This suggests a beneficial effect for constructing an efficient electroanalytical system with this new material, BP-GNMs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical activity of GCE/BP-GNMs and GCE/BP-GQDs towards X, Hx and UA
ce:section.ce:para: In further experiments, simultaneous electroanalytical detection of U, X, and Hx was conducted using GCE/BP-GNMs in pH 7 PB solution. Fig 7 A displays the CVs of GCE/BP-GNMs with increasing concentrations of a mixture of UA, X, and Hx. A systematic increase in peak currents was observed with increasing concentrations of the individual components. This observation provides primary evidence for the suitability of GCE/BP-GNMs for simultaneous electrochemical detection studies. The peak currents obtained from each component were plotted against their concentrations as inset plots in Fig. 7B-D. A linear relationship was observed, with current sensitivity values of 1.165 µA µM−1, 0.373 µA µM−1, and 0.653 µA µM−1 for UA, X, and Hx respectively in pH 7 PB solution. The peak-to-peak separation observed with the components was over 300 mV, indicating suitability for simultaneous detection. For comparison, GCE/BP-GQDs were also subjected to simultaneous detection studies, but failed to show any well-defined peak response (Fig. S5A).

To understand the electron transfer kinetics of the purine bases, a BP-GNMs modified GCE was subjected to scan rate experiments in the presence of 300 μM each of UA, X, and Hx in pH 7 PB solution, as displayed in Fig 8 A, B, and C. The GCE/BP-GNMs showed an regular increase in peak current with an increase in scan rate. A plot of anodic (i pa) peak current versus the square root of the scan rate showed a linear relationship from the origin (Fig. 8D–F). This observation indicates that the oxidation of UA, X, and Hx follows a diffusion-controlled process. Further, to understand the effect of pH on the oxidation of purine metabolites, GCE/BP-GNMs were subjected to DPVs studies in different pH solutions (pH 2–10) as in Fig. 9 A. The peak currents of UA and X initially increased from pH 2 to 6, then started to decrease. The peak current of Hx started to increase from pH 4 to 8 and then decreased. This variation in peak current is attributed to the variation in pKa values of UA, X, and Hx. All the purine bases exhibited well-defined peaks with significant current in pH 7 PB solution. The plot of Epa versus pH is displayed in Fig. 9B. The slope (∂Epa/∂pH) of the plots are; −72 (UA), −48 (X), and −47 mV pH−1 (Hx), indicating a Non-Nernstian type proton-coupled electron transfer with a ratio of 3:2 and 2:3 of H +:e − during the oxidation process. A similar kind of Non-Nernstian value was noticed in previous study with a commercial graphitized mesoporous carbon modified glassy carbon electrode in pH 7 PB solution [59]. Considering the usability of neutral pH in real samples, pH 7 PB solution was chosen as the optimal pH condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DPV for simultaneous sensing of UA, X and Hx
ce:section.ce:para: To enhance the electroanalytical signals, we adopted the DPV technique for simultaneous sensing of purine-based compounds. Two different electroanalytical studies were conducted. In Case-1, DPV was used to simultaneously sense all three purines by studying a mixture of variable concentrations. In Case-2, DPV was performed by increasing the concentration of one analyte while keeping the other two at a fixed minimal concentration. In Fig. 10 A, a typical DPV analysis of UX, X, and Hx using the Case-1 method is shown, with concentration of a mixture of the purines in pH 7 PB solution. The calculated peak currents were plotted against the respective purine concentrations, and the responses were displayed as insets in Fig. 10B-D. Linearity was observed in the concentration ranges of 15–40 µM for UA, 5–35 µM for Hx, and 15–40 µM for X, with current sensitivities of 0.9063 µA µM−1, 0.4349 µA µM−1, and 0.7178 µA µM−1, respectively. Fig. 11 A illustrates the DPV responses of the GCE/BP-GNMs examined using the Case-2 method for purine analysis. Similar to the previous case, a systematic increase in the peak current of the specific analyte was observed upon increasing the concentration of the desired individual analyte. Calibration plots for UA, X, and Hx are shown in Fig. 11B, C, and D, respectively. Although minor increments in the peak current signals were noticed with the non-spiked individuals, possibly due to interconversion of X->Hx -UA, after back ground correction, the obtained error value for the peak current was less than 5% error value. The calibration plots show linearity in the concentration window of 10–70 µM for UA, 10–70 µM for X, and 10–70 µM for Hx. Calculated sensitivity values are 328 nA µM−1 for UA, 195 nA µM−1 for X, and 272 nA µM−1 for Hx. Six repeated DPV measurements of 10 µM of UA, X, and Hx showed an RSD value of 4.1%. The limit of detection (LOD) was found to be 1.237 µM for UA, 935 nM for X, and 718 nM for Hx. The linear range, sensitivity, and detection limit of the GCE/BP-GNMs were compared with existing literature, as shown in Table 3 . The obtained electroanalytical parameters are much better than many of the commercial chemically modified electrodes; Nontronite/SPCE, SWCNT/IUTCPE, Ru(DMSO)4Cl2 Nafion/GCE, poly(bromocresol purple)/GCE, poly(pyrocatechol violet)/COOH-MWCNTs/GCE, poly(L-methionine)/GCE, PDAox–PTCA/GCE [60,38,61,39,62,40,63] and in some cases comparable [60,38,61,39,62,40,63]. These results reveal the advantage of the new graphitic material prepared by a simple and eco-friendly method for superior electroanalytical performance.

The GCE/BP-GNMs practical feasibility for sensitive detection of UA, X, and Hx in seafood (Sample #1-#3) and human urine (Sample #1) was demonstrated, as depicted in Fig. 12 . Prior to analysis, 5 mL of homogenized filtered Anchovy fish (Sample #1), mackerel fish egg (Sample #2), and shrimp (Sample #3) samples were diluted with 5 mL of pH 7 PBS. Additionally, 100 µL of the diluted fish samples were spiked into 10 mL of pH 7 PB solution and analyzed using the DPV technique with a standard addition approach. Fig. 12A and B, illustrate the DPV response of Anchovy fish (#1) and mackerel fish egg (#2) without (R) and with standard concentrations (S1—S3) of UA, X, and Hx (5, 10 and 20 µM) (Fig. 12A, B, curve R + S1—R + S3). For the urine sample analysis (#4—#6), 1 mL of the test urine sample was diluted with 9 mL of pH 7 PB solution. Subsequently, 100 µL of the sample was spiked into 10 mL of pH 7 PB solution for further DPV analysis. Fig. 12C, displays the urine real samples without (R) and with spiked standard concentrations (S; 20, 40, 60, 80, 100 µM) of UA, X, and Hx. In Fig. 12A-C (curve R), we directly extracted a real sample (without adding any UA, X, or Hx) and analysed it using DPV. The results showed only peaks corresponding to UA, X, and Hx, with no other significant peaks observed. To verify this, we performed a standard addition experiment where we gradually increased the concentration of UA, X, and Hx in the sample. The DPV responses confirmed that there were no significant interferences from other substances. Table 4 presents the electroanalytical data details for the real sample analyzed using the standard addition approach. The calculated values were comparable with literature reports of fish and human urine samples [64]. Furthermore, calculated recovery values of ∼100% highlight the suitability and applicability of the present protocol for various other real samples.

We successfully synthesized two nanomaterials from banana pulp biomass: graphene-based nanomaterials (BP-GNMs) and graphene quantum dots (BP-GQDs). BP-GNMs were simpler to produce, requiring only heating at 180 °C in air. Physicochemical analysis by TEM, SEM, FTIR, UV–Vis, XPS, and Raman spectroscopy revealed BP-GNMs to be multilayered, defective graphene sheets containing CO groups and ranging from 400 nanometers to 10 micrometers in size. Compared to BP-GQDs, BP-GNMs exhibited a more balanced ratio of sp2 (conductive) to sp3 (non-conductive) carbon sites. Additionally, BP-GNMs displayed a heterogeneous surface with a combination of core-basal and edge plane structures, leading to enhanced electrical conductivity. In contrast, BP-GQDs possessed a higher concentration of CO groups, which hindered electron transfer reaction. Consequently, electrodes modified with BP-GNMs showed superior performance in detecting purine bases (UA, X, and Hx) in urine samples compared to BP-GQDs and unmodified electrodes. The BP-GNM modified electrode distinguished each analyte with well-defined current peaks, demonstrating its potential for real-time electroanalytical applications. Furthermore, the method achieved a recovery rate of nearly 100% in real fish and human urine samples, validating its effectiveness in practical scenarios.
